data.title: RWL: A Smart Way To De-Risk Your Mega-Cap Exposure
data.description:
Many investors of the S&P 500 are worried about the high level of concentration of the index due to the market-cap weighting methodology of the index, significantly overweighting key holdings. Some ETFs are trying to take different approaches to mitigate this concentration, and the ETF that I will analyze today is one of them. The Invesco S&P 500 Revenue ETF (NYSEARCA:RWL) is weighting companies by revenues instead of market capitalization, and this results in a very different portfolio that is not taking investor sentiment into account.

I rate the fund a buy as I think it can be highly relevant for diversification purposes, however I do not think that it should be considered as a replacement for the S&P 500

A Fundamental Approach to Weighting

The Invesco S&P 500 Revenue ETF is tracking the S&P 500® Revenue-Weighted Index. The basis for the thesis of this ETF is the observation that the top constituents of the S&P 500 are becoming increasingly important in the index, and thus looking at ways to diversify weights is also becoming increasingly important, as you can see from the graph below:

By weighting companies by topline, you will see below that this is a very effective way to build a very different portfolio, while still investing in the same companies as the one you will have with the S&P 500 and an ETF such as the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), the largest ETF tracking the index and that we will use as a benchmark for this analysis. RWL takes the last twelve months sales quarterly to rebalance holdings, a strategy that keeps a close eye on companies earning reports and not on price momentum. This is an interesting strategy, but results in a portfolio favoring companies with high sales figures, not necessarily high cash generation or growth prospects, and this is what I think is more relevant as a complement than as a core holding.

The quarterly rebalancing of the portfolio results in a higher portfolio turnover than the S&P 500 despite investing in the same companies. The portfolio turnover is 15% against 3% for VOO. A high turnover typically means higher costs for the fund, but I think that 15% remains a reasonable figure, and is necessary given the strategy of the fund to keep track of every earnings in each quarter. This is consistent with the approach, and the fact that it is “only” 15% still highlights that there is a consistency in the way that the portfolio is built. RWL is not focused on the popularity of each company by investors, but by their economic footprint, and right now, the economic environment is shifting rapidly.

A Reshuffling Away from Tech Dominance

A direct consequence of the fund’s weighting methodology is the very different portfolio, both in terms of sectoral splits and individual stocks. If you have the S&P 500’s top 10 holdings in mind, you will be surprised to see the top 10 holdings of RWL:

You can clearly see that the weights are very different from the concentration of the S&P 500. The top 3 holdings are accounting for 9.84% against 20.94% for VOO: more than double. This is also true for the top 10 concentration: 24.08% against 37.85%.

If you look closely at the top 10, you will see how uniquely positioned the fund is, and how different the drivers of the fund are with regard to the S&P 500. The two largest holdings of the fund are Walmart (WMT) and Amazon (AMZN), two larg-cap companies with significative revenue metrics giving you direct exposure to American consumption and exposure ot the surge of e-commerce acitivites. They are driven by consumer spending and operational efficiency. This is followed by Health Care players UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and McKesson (MCK) and CVS Health (CVS): differents steps of the value chain. These three are reaffirming my constatation that RWL is a defensive player, as the revenue of these companies are benefiting from an inelastic demand and strong long term drivers like demographic shifts. Of course, as they are giants, you are still going to find tech players such as Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT), but in much lower weights than in the S&P 500, meaning that their performance will not be dominating the fund's outlook. This allows the fund's performance to leave rooom for cyclical value companies such as Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) and Exxon Mobil (XOM). Overall, compared to the S&P 500, it appears that the portfolio of RWL is more directly tied to the economic activity, consumer health, and healthcare trends, rather than the investor sentiment for tech companies.

The fund’s sectoral split shows that the goal of getting away from the heavy weighting on tech companies is a success. The IT weighting is lowered and this allows RWL to have larger weightings on Health Care, Consumer Staples, Financials, and Energy. This is an effective reduction from the mega-cap tech reliance and a risk-reduction because it creates a portfolio with a higher level of diversification and with lower valuation metric: a shield from the risk of leadership erosion from tech leaders and valuations compression if the economic environment deteriorates. The increased exposure to Health Care and Staples means that the portfolio has a stronger defensive and cash-generative portfolio while the stronger allocation to Financials and Energy adds some cyclicality that still allows the fund to be competitive and seek returns.

Overall, this is a more balanced portfolio that can be used to complement the S&P 500 as it will reduce the drawdown risk, diversify the sources of earnings and have the potential to benefit from margin expansions over fundamentals in different sectors.

Value, Performance, and Risk

Now, as we compare RWL and VOO, you will see more in detail why I don’t think that it should be a core holding, as the value tilt of RWL doesn't have only benefits.

Looking at valuation metrics, there is indeed a clear appeal with RWL’s strategy, a consequence of the sectoral split. The fund’s P/E ratio is 19.11 and its P/B ratio is 2.91, a lot lower than VOO’s 27.7 and 5.0. This is why I say that RWL is more of a value investment, for a dollar invested in the fund you get higher earnings and book value. However, as the fund is not implementing other quality screenings besides revenues, the risk is that the fund focuses on value traps: companies with low growth prospects, and maybe profitability issues. This is partly offset by the fact that the investment universe is the S&P 500, companies that should be the best among all U.S. equities.

To buy RWL and its niche strategy, you are going to pay a higher expense ratio: 0.39%. This is a very important cost to my eyes, and a wide gap with VOO’s 0.03%. This is a 0.36 percentage points additional fee that you pay for the diversification. This higher fee is partly offset by the dividend distribution of 1.39%, 0.22 percentage points above VOO’s dividend and backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 1.46%. The dividend appears to be resilient enough, and even has the potential to be increased, which would be welcomed to mitigate the higher expense ratio.

Now, the most important thing is the performance. The market is driven by growth tech companies: is RWL’s positioning relevant enough to provide investors with returns?

As you can see, the performance of RWL is overall lower than the one of VOO, except for 5Y returns. What I like to see is that the volatility of the total returns is clearly lower, and this strengthens the appeal of the fund as a risk mitigation tool. However, the lower returns also mean that there is an opportunity cost in owning RWL, which is why I don’t recommend it as a core holding: RWL is not for performance, but to address risks.

One clear way of seeing this is by looking at risk-adjusted returns. Sharpe ratios are 1.04 (3 years), 0.89 (5 years), and 0.75 (10 years) against 1.38 (3 years), 0.85 (5 years), and 0.87 (10 years) for VOO: lower. The reason behind that is purely the performance, and the fact that despite a very different positioning, the fund’s volatility is almost the same than the one of VOO: standard deviations are 13.90% (3 years), 15.78% (5 years), and 15.61% (10 years) against 13.37% (3 years), 15.84% (5 years), and 15.31% (10 years). With this now in mind, you might challenge my idea that RWL is interesting to mitigate risks. Actually, when I say that, I am not mentioning the daily volatility, I am mentioning the fund’s behavior during market downturns.

Indeed, if you look at the fund’s maximum drawdown over the past 5 years, you will clearly see the benefit of investing away from high valuation tech giants. The fund’s maximum drawdown is only -16.01%, against -23.90%: a good way to preserve capital while still benefiting from interesting returns.

Risks

Liquidity risk: the liquidity of the fund is adequate with a median bid/ask spread of 0.03%, but this comes with an average daily trading of $17.11 Mn, a lot lower than VOO’s $5.7 Bn. Of course this remains reasonable but an institutional investor interested in the fund is going to drive price movements and widen the gap while it makes a transaction, and with an S&P 500 ETF this is something to take into account, in addition to the already higher expense ratio.

Value Trap Risk: I mentioned it earlier, but I think that it is the core risk of this ETF. The revenue weighting methodology doesn’t come with significant screening to ensure that the weights of companies are relevant with the growth prospects or profitability of the companies selected. The fund would benefit from additional filters like profitability, cash flow, or debt situations. While the S&P 500 average quality of companies offsets this risk, the traditional market-cap weighting uses “the wisdom of the market” as a way to filter this kind of companies.

Underperformance: This ETF is not well suited to provide investors with significant returns in growth driven and high momentum markets, and this is why it is underperforming VOO. For as long as market gains are dictated by a few leading companies, owning RWL comes with a strong opportunity cost that might not be offset by the risk diversification that the fund is giving you for some investors.

Conclusion

I rate the Invesco S&P 500 Revenue ETF as a buy, not as a standalone investment, but because I think that it can be a relevant option for some investors to diversify the market-cap exposure of their S&P 500 investment. The revenue weighting methodology is an efficient way to diminish the IT weight in the S&P 500 and mitigate the drawdown risk without sacrificing returns too much. I don’t expect RWL to have the potential to outperform in the long run, but for risk management purposes, this is an effective option.

data.title: ASML: Solid Q3, Limited Room For Upside
data.description:
ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) has raised since my July note, shares are up ~33%, reflecting AI-led EUV momentum and a risk-on tape. The thesis remains the same: near-term prints look strong, but order visibility into 2026 is limited as China normalizes and High-NA ramps from a small base. At today’s valuation, I still see the risk-reward as balanced and maintain my Hold rating

ASML Q3 Earnings Call

Q3 delivered solid execution: net sales €7.5B, gross margin 51.6%, net income €2.1B, and bookings €5.4B (of which €3.6B EUV). Results were broadly in line; gross margin and EPS were a touch ahead, and bookings modestly beat. The mix included one High-NA system recognition and ~€2.0B of installed base revenue. Management framed demand as AI-driven on the leading edge (advanced logic/DRAM) with a more cautious tone into 2026 as China steps down.

Guidance implies a record Q4’25 revenue of €9.2–€9.8B and GM of 51–53%, taking FY25 to ~€32.5B at ~52% GM, consistent with the company’s back-end-loaded cadence. Management reiterated that while EUV intensity is rising, 2026 is still a transition year: EUV up, DUV down, with overall 2026 sales "not below" 2025 as things stand.

Operationally, the High-NA ramp continues to progress (300k+ wafers processed at customers; first DRAM production install underway), and ASML shipped the XT:260 into advanced packaging (3D integration), adding a small but strategic long-tail TAM.

Beyond results, ASML announced a strategic partnership and an 11% stake in Mistral AI, aiming to strengthen software capabilities in lithography and inspection. I don’t love hardware companies investing in software, but it’s becoming the norm. This can add some speculative upside, though I’d rather see that capital reinvested in the core business or returned to shareholders.

My take

China sales are expected to fall further in 2026, following the 15–20% decline in 2025. That’s the normalization I’ve been expecting for some time, as demand from China has been very high since 2023, especially on the DUV front. To achieve any growth in 2026, EUV and services likely need to grow at least around 10%. It’s feasible, but management’s tone doesn’t really point that way yet.

The share price appreciation since July has outpaced fundamental revisions. ASML now trades at forward multiples that still carry a premium.

Metric ASML Sector Median 5Y Avg Δ vs. 5Y Avg
P/E (FWD, Non-GAAP) 35.3× 25.3× 38.3× –7.9%
P/E (FWD, GAAP) 36.5× 33.0× 38.5× –5.2%

I’ve argued for multiple contraction because I don’t think High-NA will drive the same growth over EUV that EUV did over DUV. That premium did shrink as expected, but we’re now seeing expansion across players as risk-on comes back.

If we take markets consensus (Capital IQ S&P) projections to apply on a DCF framework, using 9.8%* as the discount factor, we'd have the following:

CY2025E CY2026E CY2027E CY2028E
Revenue (€B) 32.35 33.78 37.94 42.89
EPS (€) 24.20 25.54 30.45 35.50
Operating Cash Flow (€M) 6,992 11,252 12,501 17,019
Capex (€M) (1,811) (1,932) (2,062) (2,156)
Free Cash Flow (€M) 5,181 9,320 10,439 14,863
DCF (per share) €668.56 / $775.53
P/E 2026 (normalized) $1,056.40
Sector P/E (normalized) $698.39
Average (blended fair value) $915.97

The company is trading near its historical forward multiple, which leaves little room for upside unless EPS turn out significantly higher, something I don’t expect, especially given management’s tone.

From now on, I’ll be using a proprietary method to calculate the discount rate, considering a 4.5% equity risk premium, the U.S. 10-year rate, and additional penalties based on company size, revenue volatility over the past five years, overall scale, and currency exposure.

Upside risks

• Intel getting fully operational and growing at the leading edge could make me more constructive, as it would add a third major EUV buyer alongside TSM and Samsung. The U.S. government seems to be working toward that.

• A significant near- to mid-term capex lift at TSM could also imply more sales, but I believe that would already be reflected in bookings, so there’s limited room for surprise

Conclusion

Q3 confirms robust near-term fundamentals and record Q4 momentum, but the debate still turns on what 2026 looks like. With the stock up ~33% since July and trading near my blended fair value, I maintain Hold, awaiting either firmer 2026 visibility or a better entry point

data.title: Micron: Growth Is Not Over Yet
data.description:
You know that in the development of microelectronics, the main core is, of course, the chip in the form of a CPU, a GPU, or a custom ASIC chip. And, of course, it is this market that attracts the most attention from investors. However, I suggest paying attention to another very important market for the development of microelectronics and AI. This is the DRAM, HBM, and NAND Flash memory market. And this market is huge. According to various estimates, it is worth about $200 billion a year. This market is divided between three major players.

They are the Korean companies SK Hynix and Samsung, and the American company Micron (NASDAQ:MU). It is Micron that I will evaluate in this article.

Volatile Cyclical Demand

Micron has a unique history; its past performance has been dependent on cyclical demand in the memory market. I will try to describe this in simple terms.

In short, at a certain point, the technology industry took a step forward, and a new generation of devices appeared on the consumer market—computers, smartphones, tablets, and the like. Naturally, demand for gadgets increased, followed by demand for memory modules. But as the market became saturated, the wave of consumer growth stopped and paused until the next round of technological development. And, as you can imagine, demand for memory modules declined as a result.

Micron's financial performance followed these unpredictable cycles. This unpredictability prevented the company from generating stable cash flow and profits. And as I understand it, this prevented investors from valuing the company highly. In the table below, you can see these fluctuating profit and revenue figures by year.

The table shows how peak profits in 2018 of $14 billion plummeted to $2.7 billion by 2020. This was followed by growth until 2022, and then another decline.

Nevertheless, Micron is one of the three market leaders in memory production, and its share in the DRAM market, for example, is estimated at 22%. Korean companies SK Hynix and Samsung surpass Micron in terms of volume.

Unlike the processor market, the DRAM and NAND memory market is effectively an oligopoly of these three companies. They all have their own factories, and there is no analogue to TSMC that could produce memory to order based on drawings. Any new player who wants to compete in this industry will have to build the entire infrastructure for themselves, which is very difficult and expensive. And I think this is a very strong barrier for other participants.

Western Protectionism

Since the memory market involves both engineering and manufacturing within a single company, as far as I understand, Korean companies have overtaken Micron thanks to cheaper labor and the overall presence of a powerful manufacturing ecosystem in Asia. But as you probably already know, in recent years, the West has finally realized its vulnerability in the semiconductor supply chain, which has led to the emergence of the CHIPS Act, whose main goal is to bring chip manufacturing back to American soil for the sake of national security.

And I believe that Micron is the main beneficiary of this strategy in the memory sector. The company has already received a $6.1 billion grant to build its mega-factories in Idaho and New York. But the point here is not even that the company is receiving money. Not at all. Koreans will also build their factories in the US and are already doing so. The main thing, as I see it, is something else.

When Korean companies move their production to the US, they lose their main “Asian” advantage. They face the same reality as Micron. High wages in the US, US regulations, and the absence of the industrial ecosystem they are accustomed to. This brings to mind the idea that this levels the playing field in terms of their production capabilities. But considering that this is a familiar environment for Micron, they even gain an advantage over the Koreans.

Therefore, I think the CHIPS Act is not just a plus for Micron. It further changes the competitive landscape with the Koreans, strengthening the company's position.

AI Is Changing The Rules Of The Game

But let's get to the point. In recent years, the development of the AI industry has, in my opinion, completely changed the memory market. AI servers require more and more memory. This market is growing and creating new demand for memory devices.

A new type of memory for the AI revolution has appeared on the memory market: HBM (High Bandwidth Memory). This memory has a new architecture, where the entire circuit is no longer located on a single plane, but has multiple layers connected by vertical links, essentially creating a 3D architecture. The market for this type of memory was estimated at $4.3 billion in 2024, and by 2035, it is projected to grow to $30 billion. Importantly, Micron has a very strong position here. Their latest product, HBM3E, is one of the best in the world. According to the company's own data, it not only provides the highest speed (over 9.2 Gb/s), but also 30% better energy efficiency compared to competitors. Micron's HBM3E memory has become a key component for NVIDIA's latest and most powerful accelerators — the H200 and the upcoming Blackwell platform (B200, GB200).

I didn't describe this technology here for no reason. I simply believe that the level of technological development is very important for a company in the technology industry. And the fact that Micron has this product allows me to conclude that the company is doing well in terms of technology.

I would also like to draw your attention to the fact that the growing and stable demand for memory modules from the AI industry is radically changing the memory market itself. I believe that the cyclical nature of this industry is now a thing of the past. Demand from the AI industry will smooth out fluctuations in consumer market demand. This means that Micron's financial performance will not only grow in the future, but also become more stable. And financial stability implies a higher valuation multiple compared to what it was in the past.

The company's quarterly results, as you can see in the table below, already show the dynamics of stable and sustainable growth. And I think, in view of what I have said above, this dynamic will continue.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I would like to say that Micron has suffered from cyclical demand for decades. But now there are signs that this destructive cyclicality will come to an end. Moreover, the memory market has gained an additional fast-growing source of demand from the AI industry. And Western countries' protectionism regarding chip production is not only a source of additional money, but also creates conditions for improving Micron's position among its competitors.

The company's financial indicators are already growing. But the company's shares have also grown approximately twofold over the year. Against this backdrop, it may seem to many that prices have already taken everything into account.

But I would like to draw your attention to the fact that in 2021, as in early 2025, the company's shares were trading at around $100 per share. At a time when the entire technology market was growing, Micron's shares lagged behind the market due to the risks of business cyclicality. I think that the growth of recent months to almost $200 has only covered some of the advantages I described in the article, but not all of them. Therefore, I believe that the company's shares still have growth potential. And that is why I would give the company's shares a buy recommendation.

Risks

However, there are always risks. The main risk is the possibility of a correction in the US market. I believe this is the most likely risk, and I wrote about my concerns about the market in general in one of my articles. But despite my concerns about the market as a whole, I think Micron has a very strong track record that could drive its shares higher.

Of course, in a competitive market, there is always the technological risk that competitors will come up with a new product that will allow them to dominate and squeeze out others.

And in the future, there is a risk that all three companies, SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron, which have already started building new factories, will build excess capacity, which will again bring this market into a cyclical state.

data.title: If I Could Own Just 5 Stocks, These Would Be It
data.description:
Introduction

Some of my favorite investors have one thing in common, which is that they maintain extremely concentrated portfolios. For example, Chris Hohn of TCI Fund Management has 24% of his investments in GE Aerospace (GE), which is also one of my biggest holdings. On top of that, he has 17% exposure to Microsoft (MSFT), 14% exposure to Visa (V), and a combined 25% exposure to Moody’s (MCO) and its peer S&P Global (SPGI).

His biggest five holdings account for nearly 70% of his $70 billion fund.

Meanwhile, Bill Ackman has 21% of his capital in Uber Technologies (UBER), followed by 19% in Brookfield Corp. (BN). That’s 40% in just two holdings. When adding his third-largest investment, Restaurant Brands International (QSR), which is the parent company of Burger King, his total exposure rises beyond 50%.

According to his own shareholder letter, they keep it very straightforward, as they focus on a number of high-conviction investments that account for most of their investment volume and are unlikely to be changed on a regular basis:

The substantial majority of the Company’s portfolio is typically allocated to 8 to 12 core holdings usually comprising liquid, listed large capitalization North American companies. The Investment Manager generally focuses on high-quality growth businesses, which it believes have limited downside and that generate predictable, recurring cash flows, and in certain cases seeks to catalyze managerial, operating and governance changes as levers to create substantial, enduring and long-term shareholder value.

The Investment Manager aims to manage risks through careful investment selection and portfolio construction, engaging in typically only one to three new core investments each year. - Pershin Square Holdings

And then there’s Murray Stahl, the founder of Horizon Kinetics. His personal portfolio shows 3.6 million shares of Texas Pacific Land (TPL) as his biggest investment, pushing the value to $3.8 billion, or 45% of his entire portfolio. When adding his investments in Bitcoin (15%), his biggest two holdings account for 60% of his personal net worth.

So far, it seems these billionaires follow the advice of Shark Tank’s Mark Cuban, who made quite a few headlines last year when he said diversification is for idiots:

Contrary to the widely accepted principle of diversification, Cuban provocatively claimed, "Diversification, that's for idiots." He argued that spreading investments across different assets is ineffective without specific market knowledge. Instead, Cuban advocates for concentrated bets based on thorough research. - Yahoo Finance

Obviously, I’m not telling you this to make the case that diversification is bad. In fact, there is plenty of evidence that suggests diversification works. For example, the chart below was produced by Meir Statman (2002), who found that adding just a handful of stocks has a massive impact on the standard deviation of a portfolio, as we can see below.

While their research found that we may need to own more than 100 individual stocks, their findings (see below) also show that once we own more than 25 companies, the benefits of adding more uncorrelated investments aren’t that substantial anymore.

The reason I’m bringing all of this up is because I promised to tell readers what the five stocks are that I would own in a super concentrated portfolio. As most of my readers may know, I own a concentrated portfolio of 16 stocks.

While going down to just five stocks would be nuts, picking five stocks is still a task that could add value:

  • Picking a small number of stocks in a fictional scenario forces us to review all of our existing holdings as well as our watchlist. Why would you include certain stocks? Why are some stocks not included? These are important questions that can help us keep a tight ship when it comes to our wealth.
  • I have often said that if I had a different job, I would own a mix of ETFs and stocks. My best friend, for example, who loves investing but works in an unrelated field of finance, has a strategy of keeping 50% in ETFs and 50% in single stocks. By doing so, he keeps excellent diversification, while also consistently buying his highest-conviction ideas. It’s an approach I really like for a wide range of investors.

If I had a 50/50 strategy, I would likely own one high-conviction ETF that fits my current strategy. Right now, that focus is more on dividend growth than dividend income. A few decades from now (God willing), that focus could shift to income. Right now, if I had to pick one ETF (and if I had access to American ETFs), I would likely pick the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV), which may have a somewhat high expense ratio of 0.33%, but also comes with a five-star Morningstar rating.

As we can see in the overview below, it has a well-diversified portfolio (of 52 companies with a weighted average yield of 1.4%), with no position accounting for more than 6.2% of its total weighting. This includes technology, fast-growing healthcare, consumer staples, and many companies with fascinating secular growth stories like GE Aerospace, RTX Corp. (RTX), and NVIDIA (NVDA).

Now, the second part is about the five companies I would pick if I could own just five single companies, whether it’s in an all-single stock portfolio or in a 50/50 constellation.

In other words, I will give you five of my favorite picks right now from my actual portfolio and my watchlist.

So, let’s get to it!

Picking The Best 5 Stocks Money Can Buy

The most obvious statement of this article will be that when we look to build a concentrated portfolio, we need to have a focus on quality. This means wide-moat business models that are hard to disrupt, potentially secular tailwinds or other benefits that future-proof these business models, and strong track records of shareholder value creation.

Add to that list factors like a healthy balance sheet to reduce financial risks. Also, and this is a bit tricky to implement for some, I like companies that benefit from more than one customer group for diversification purposes.

It’s tricky because it may get some people to end up buying conglomerates. That’s not my point. For example, I own The Home Depot (HD). This DIY retailer has both consumer and PRO exposure, which allows me to get exposure to the American consumer and the housing market, including remodeling and new construction, through professional customers requiring tools and equipment.

That’s why The Home Depot is my first pick, as it allows me to buy both consumer and professional housing operations under the roof of one of the best-managed companies in North America (or anywhere in the world) with an A-rated balance sheet.

For example, last year, it bought SRS Distribution in a deal with an enterprise value of more than $18 billion. This deal increased the company’s total addressable market by $50 billion to roughly $1 trillion. Last year, Home Depot had a share of 17% in that market, which now allows it to better cater to PRO customers. In fact, because of its massive store network, it is in a great spot to quickly serve both PRO and retail demand, using improvements like same-day delivery and buy online and pick up in store measures.

It also has a decent dividend yield of 2.5% with a track record of 15 consecutive annual hikes. It is protected by a 62% payout ratio and comes with a five-year CAGR of 9.3%. This year, EPS is expected to be down 2% due to consumer weakness and a locked housing market. However, in the FY2027 and FY2028 years, EPS growth is expected to be 9% and 10%, respectively. I expect higher growth rates due to my favorable view of housing.

My second pick is the Texas Pacific Land Corporation, which is one of the companies Murray Stahl and I have in common, although with a huge difference in total exposure, unfortunately.

I discussed this fascinating company in a recent article, as it brings so many qualities to the table that I cannot ignore it. So, yes, I am fully aware that I bring it up a lot, so I keep this short (I promise). For starters, it owns close to 900,000 acres in the Permian Basin, America’s most critical oil basin. It produces roughly as much oil as the other non-Permian basins combined.

I am including Texas Pacific Land because it offers a wide range of benefits. For starters, roughly half of its revenues come from oil and gas royalties, which it gets in exchange for allowing drillers to produce on its land. These are ultra-high-margin royalties, as the company has no drilling expenses. This also adds a thick layer of inflation protection, as it benefits from potentially rising energy prices without the negative consequences of rising costs.

Nearly 40% of its revenue comes from water operations, which include providing fresh water for the drilling process and produced water operations.

Produced water is toxic water as a byproduct of the drilling process, as most drillers encounter more than four barrels of produced water per barrel of produced oil. These water volumes are rapidly rising as drillers have to drill deeper. That’s why these revenues have risen exponentially despite massively slowing oil production in the Permian.

While TPL yields less than 1.0%, it doesn't have a penny in gross debt, ultra-high margins, and benefits like oil and gas exposure, a booming water business, and the increasing commercial opportunities on its land, like crypto mines, solar farms, and much more.

So far, we have consumer cyclical exposure and a diversified high-margin energy company. Now, I am adding an industrial company to the mix, which is so "mission-critical" that without it, our cities and communities would be in serious trouble. It’s also the first pick that I do not own yet, as it’s a name very high on my watchlist.

That company is Republic Services (RSG), one of the two public waste management companies that dominate the United States in the $165 billion environmental services industry that has an 80% annuity-type profile, making it very resilient and predictable.

RSG, which yields 1.1% with a super healthy payout ratio of a mere 34%, has hiked its dividend for more than 20 consecutive years with a five-year CAGR of 7.6%. The best thing, however, is that it serves a market that not only has an 80% annuity profile but also strong pricing, as it saw 6.5% core pricing last year, supported by 60% open market pricing.

Although its business has a cyclical aspect due to the fact that faster economic growth and a strong consumer/housing market generate faster growth in waste, their business model is very resilient and a very likely winner from secular growth in a tight housing market, renewable trends, population growth, and what I expect to become a bigger focus on natural gas from waste, as it is increasingly turning waste into cash.

The company, which has an A- credit rating from S&P Global, has returned 18.2% since the end of 2014. And while its dividend yield may be low, an initial investment of $10,000 back then would have resulted in more than $4,400 worth of dividends, 44% of the initial investment.

Going forward, analysts (data collected by FactSet) expect close to 10% annual EPS growth, a number I expect to last for many years to come.

While RSG isn’t necessarily cheap at 33.3x earnings, above its ten-year average of 26.5x, I don’t expect RSG to get cheap anytime soon, as the market knows it offers the kind of safety that is hard to come by. That’s why it’s on my watchlist.

The next pick adds financial exposure. It’s a company I own. That company is CME Group (CME), a company, I believe, that fits nearly every single (dividend) investing strategy you can think of, as it offers a great mix of growth and value with a super wide business moat.

Investing in CME Group is like buying a Las Vegas casino, with the one big exception being that CME Group doesn’t deal in entertainment but the world’s most important futures exchanges. This includes CBOT, NYMEX, CME, and many other platforms where investors and traders have access to the world’s most liquid interest rate, energy, equity, agriculture, forex, and metal futures and options you can think of.

This includes the famous E-mini S&P 500 futures, backed by exclusive licensing agreements and a stake in S&P Dow Jones Indices that support its wide business moat. This business model allows investors to make money every time someone trades these futures and related options, which also means that during times of elevated volatility, the company tends to see higher ADVs (average daily volumes).

Since 2015, it has grown its ADVs by 7% per year, allowing it to boost its net income by 12% per year. The best thing is that the company was able to keep growing consistently after the massive pandemic-related volatility tailwinds, which is supported by new products like futures on crypto, niche metals, and others.

The best thing isn’t just that the company’s size allows it to offer attractive products at very attractive rates, which protects it against competitors (see slide 9), but also that its business is easy to scale. After all, it works closely with Google Cloud and works almost entirely online. This means it can add new services by simply expanding its online capabilities.

As we can see below, last year, it turned $6.1 billion worth of revenue into $3.7 billion of net income. That’s a net margin of 61%. This makes it the fourth-most profitable company in the S&P 500, one step below Texas Pacific Land, the third-most profitable company in the index.

With regard to its dividend, the company pays four quarterly dividends and one special dividend to return nearly all of its free cash flow to shareholders. This is possible because of its double-A-rated balance sheet and because it does not need substantial capital to grow.

Although that may change in the future if the company ever decides it needs M&A to grow (there is no indication of that right now), it yields close to 4.5%, which is a great yield for a company that is expected to grow its EPS in the mid-to-high single-digit range in the years ahead. Any unforeseen volatility events could substantially boost these numbers.

As a fifth pick, I initially wanted to go with Blue Owl Capital (OWL), the asset manager behind the Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC) BDC and one of the fastest-growing private credit platforms in the world.

I’m bringing it up because buying OWL would add a credit platform of nearly $150 billion to the portfolio, close to $70 billion in strategic capital through minority stakes in other companies, and more than $71 billion in real assets, including nearly 5,800 assets, most of which are operated on a net lease basis.

Buying this business would add real estate and lending from a company that is currently growing its fee-related earnings by more than 20%. I also considered Blackstone (BX) for the same reasons.

However, I made it a wild card, as I decided to go with Union Pacific (UNP), a company I simply couldn’t resist, as it owns real estate that is far more valuable than anything Owl owns. By that, I mean its rail network that covers all economic hotspots and supply chains in the Western two-thirds of the United States, connections to Canada and Mexico, and the opportunity to connect the entire U.S. through the pending merger with Norfolk Southern (NSC).

This merger, which has the support of the president, would create the first transcontinental railroad with massive exposure in consumer-focused intermodal, industrial goods, and agriculture. At this pace, the company expects the deal to be closed in early 2027 and create a railroad with room to grow its free cash flow by 60% in the first three years through synergies, allowing it to rapidly reduce debt and protect its A-rated balance sheet.

Currently, UNP yields 2.5% with a sub-50% payout ratio, a five-year CAGR of 6.8%, and a track record of 18 consecutive years, amplified by aggressive buybacks (see below).

Although its stock price has been sluggish since 2021, this is mainly due to cyclical weakness, something I expect to reverse in the next few quarters, as I discussed in articles like this one.

While there may be industrial companies with more growth than UNP, in a hyper-concentrated portfolio (or a 50/50 strategy), it provides the widest moat of all companies mentioned in this article and a business model I expect to generate tremendous value for many decades to come.

I simply had to make it one of the five picks.

That said, another close contender was Quanta Services (PWR), which serves the infrastructure needs of utilities, midstream companies, and other mission-critical players. I do not own it, but I would love to add it to my portfolio later this year.

Takeaway

As I wrote in the intro, I am always fascinated by how some of the world’s best investors don’t stick to the usual diversification rules/suggestions. This makes sense, as they are professionals, while it may not be a great strategy for beginners. However, even if you entirely disagree with this approach, it’s still a fun exercise that allows us to assess our current holdings and the stocks on our watchlist.

That’s why I presented five (and some wild card picks) ideas that I wouldn’t mind holding in a 5-stock portfolio or in a portfolio with a foundation of diversified ETFs.

All of these are my highest-conviction ideas that I will ideally all own in the not-too-distant future.

They have wide-moat business models, secular tailwinds, and opportunities to deliver elevated long-term shareholder returns, all supported by healthy balance sheets.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risks I need to mention here are that any company I mentioned can run into trouble. As wide as a moat may be, there’s always a disruption risk. Also, general recession and market risks apply, as these could pressure all the companies I mentioned.

Before you buy any of the companies I mentioned, please make sure to do your own due diligence and assess if they fit your strategy. This is a food-for-thought article, not an attempt to get you to go overboard or buy these exact five companies.

data.title: Panic Or Opportunity? 2 Income Stocks I'd Dollar-Cost Average Into Now
data.description:
Introduction

The market has continued to trend higher despite the government shutdown and macro uncertainty. However, if you search long and hard enough, there are some quality stocks worth buying right now.

Especially if you're an income-focused investor. And while the market could suffer a correction in the near term, taking advantage of companies that may be undervalued is a great way to see strong future returns. And this definitely holds true if you plan on holding for the long term.

In this article, I discuss two stocks investors should consider if they're looking for income.

#1 VICI Properties Inc. (VICI)

VICI Properties is one of my favorite stocks I currently own. And if you're looking for income in a frothy market, then you should consider them as well. I covered them two months ago, assigning the REIT a Hold rating because of a potential pullback due to macro uncertainty.

Since, the stock is down over 6% and trades less around $31 a share. Personally, I think the stock is attractive here at a forward P/AFFO multiple of 13.12x. This is below the sector median's 14.73x.

And because of their quality, I think VICI deserves to trade at a minimum in line with its peer average. During their latest earnings, the REIT reported strong [earnings], with their top line crossing $1 billion. This represented a growth rate of 4.6% year-over-year.

AFFO grew a solid 6.4% in total, from $592.4 million to $630.2 million. Even in a state of uncertainty surrounding the economy, interest rates, and declining Las Vegas tourism, VICI raised full-year AFFO guidance for the second time.

This represents a growth rate of 4.4% from the prior year. VICI also raised its dividend to $0.45 a share. And their forward dividend yield of 5.81% is well-covered with a payout ratio of 64.77%.

VICI also has one of the most solid balance sheets in the sector, with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.1x. This declined from 5.4x the year prior, and liquidity was robust with $2.9 billion available.

Although the stock has been volatile over the past month, I'm confident that they see solid upside if interest rates continue to decline as expected.

Safer investments like money markets and high-yield savings accounts will likely yield much lower in the next year. And VICI should benefit as investors rotate back into the market looking for higher yields.

I expect VICI Properties to break through the $34-$35 level and perhaps even get closer to $40 a share.

#2 Ares Capital (ARCC)

Second stock that could be considered attractive if you're looking for income is Ares Capital. Although business development companies, or BDCs, could fall further if interest rates continue to decline, the stock currently trades below its net asset value, indicating the selloff may be overdone.

I last covered ARCC back in August, assigning them a Hold rating due to lower rates. In the past month alone, ARCC has fallen close to 16%. While this is likely due to lower interest rates, I didn't expect the sector to fall as quickly as it did.

I expect many of the high-quality BDCs like Ares Capital to see volatility around the December/January time frame. And I think the market is pricing in not only multiple rate cuts but also a potential dividend cut for ARCC as well.

ARCC's dividend coverage fell from 129% to 102% during their last quarter. And although the BDC has size & scale advantages plus robust liquidity levels, a dividend cut in the next 1 to 3 months is plausible.

ARCC had $1.29 in spillover income plus $6.5 billion in available liquidity. But I believe many BDC management teams are willing to reduce their dividends should yields continue falling.

And this is because even with reductions, most will still have high starting dividend yields. While spillover income can temporarily cover dividend shortfalls, maturing debt and tighter spreads will also weigh on dividend coverage.

ARCC's balance sheet was solid, with leverage well below the peer average at 1.01x. But with over $2 billion in debt maturing next year, I think this puts pressure on the dividend. And as mentioned previously, I think the market may be pricing this in as their P/NAV is attractive at 0.95x.

For the last 3 years, ARCC traded at a premium to NAV with the exception of the April Liberation Day sell-off. While the stock could fall further due to BDC interest rate sensitivity, I think they're worth starting a position here and dollar-cost averaging.

And while there are other BDCs with higher yields and more attractive valuations, ARCC's size and scale advantages make them a better option for long-term income investors.

Wall Street currently rates them a Buy with a price target of $23.14. I don't see significant upside unless the Fed decides to do a reversal and raise interest rates to battle inflation. But their double-digit yield and a potential market overreaction to lower interest rates could be a buying opportunity.

Volatility Risks

VICI Properties and Ares Capital are both sensitive to interest rates. With the Fed lowering rates last month, REITs should be seeing capital appreciation instead of being in the red. But this has nothing to do with their fundamentals and more to do with uncertainty around inflation.

As a result, long-term treasury yields could remain volatile short-term, resulting in potential downside from here. But as mentioned previously, I expect VICI to move closer to the $35–$40 range in the next year or two.

Regarding ARCC, further downside is likely. But at a P/NAV ratio below 1x for a BDC of their caliber, I think their double-digit yield and advantages in size & scale make them worth DCA'ing into at their price below NAV.

Investor Takeaway

With recent volatility in sectors sensitive to interest rates, the REIT (XLRE) and BDC (BIZD) sectors present some attractive buying opportunities currently.

I expect some volatility short-term that could even result in further downside for both companies.

But at valuations below the peer average and their net asset value, dollar-cost averaging into two top stocks in their respective sectors should be viewed as buying opportunities.

If you're looking for income from two stocks I consider to be great long-term holdings, consider VICI Properties and Ares Capital for your portfolio.

data.title: TCHP: Top-Heavy SPY Means This ETF Is Just Another One Of Many
data.description:
What Is TCHP?

The T. Rowe Price Blue Chip Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:TCHP) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) that invests in a concentrated portfolio of mostly large-cap American listed stocks with the hope of capital appreciation. T. Rowe Price is pitching that they have the potential to outperform the broad market S&P 500 index by investing in companies that are well established in their industries and have the potential for above-average earnings growth. The fund aims to focus on companies with leading market positions, seasoned management, and strong financial fundamentals.

Over the last several years, the fund has done ok, but has employed higher fees. I recently wrote about TCHP and noted that I didn't like how the fund managers had heavy tech holdings, like MSFT and NVDA, alongside higher fees. If funds invest in hard-to-access assets, like private equity, or a unique portfolio of stocks, I don't mind the fees. But it seems like the fund has just been replicating SPY with heavier weightings in tech and slapped an actively managed label on the fund as justification to charge higher fees.

Since the Liberation Day scare, TCHP initially roared back, easily beating its benchmark by ~8%, along with creating some true diversity in its holdings with some unique winners. After reviewing the fund facts and commentary from management, I changed this fund to a Hold because the outperformance has impressed me, and it's worth watching this summer.

That said, since my article in June, it seems like the top heavy nature of SPY has caught up to TCHP. While their original strategy of owning larger weightings in tech heavy names boosted early returns, SPY caught up and the fund has traded similarly to the cheap passive large cap index. In general, I remain skeptical of many actively managed ETFs, and it's part of the rationale of why I'm continuing to write about which ETF's stack up better against their competitors. Often times, investors are trying to get exposure to a certain industry, but many times are faced with terrible options that underperform with high fees. Passively managed means fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, compared with TCHP's managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity.

After further review, TCHP has seen its strategy become muted over the past few months, as SPY has top concentrations of NVDA, MSFT and the rest of the top 10 stocks that make up around 40% of the portfolio. While the bounce back after Liberation Day was swift, TCHP has aligned with the market returns recently, making me feel like they should be doing more to earn their actively managed fees.

Fund Breakdown

TCHP launched on August 4, 2020 by Fidelity with a mandate to invest at least 80% of its net assets (plus any borrowings for investment purposes) in securities of blue-chip companies with growth characteristics. T. Rowe Price also noted in their prospectus to investors that they "may have a significant portion of its assets invested in the same economic sector, such as the information technology sector". The idea of the fund is to narrow the focus on "blue chip" companies with leading market positions, seasoned management teams, strong financial conditions and above-average growth. The fund has a NAV of ~$1.4Bn and is liquid, trading around 100,000 shares a day worth ~$5MM. Ultimately, T. Rowe Price was basically noting that they were launching a fund to track the S&P 500, and be more actively managed to generate additional alpha from those returns, using internal risk-managed tools for their asset managers.

Upon further review of the ETF's results, it's unclear that the fund has achieved what it set out to do. Below is a chart over the past five years (since launch) showing poor results versus the S&P 500, but with significantly larger declines in bear markets. In the past five years, the fund hasn't been able to significantly beat the passive benchmark - and over the past few months, its followed about the same pattern as SPY.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When reviewing TCHP's recent portfolio, there are some differences in the portfolio breakdown from its benchmark. As of Aug. 31, 2025, the portfolio has some higher concentrations in MSFT, NVDA, AAPL, and AMZN, which make up ~44% of the portfolio. The fund has also weighted CVNA much higher than SPY, which I will note has been a great call by the fund managers, as the stock has soared 73%+ YTD.

Risks

From a risk perspective, the fund has a beta of 1.07 which means that the fund will have more volatility than the benchmark – and that has borne out in past results. The R-squared value that indicates how much of a fund’s fluctuations were attributable to movements in the fund’s benchmark index was 76, which is reasonable for an actively managed fund. Additionally, the Sharpe ratio has jumped with the market, so TCHP has definitely had a good stretch. The fund has also seen liquidity jump, but I would caution that it's still not anywhere near as liquid as SPY, VT, or other large cap ETFs.

Conclusion

TCHP is an actively managed ETF that continues to track the S&P 500, and recently has met its benchmark. The fees of 0.57% are a bit steep, and the fund has underperformed over the past few years. But since Liberation Day, the fund has done well, and managers have had some good picks, notably CVNA. While U.S. equities could face increased volatility from tariff uncertainty, I'm keeping this fund at a Hold and if investors want a tech-heavy large cap fund, this is at least worth adding to the watch list after the last couple of months. I don't think it's a top ETF in the large cap space, notably given the low portfolio turnover and lack of differentiated strategy, but it's been ok.

data.title: BKV Corporation: This Natural Gas Producer Is Attractively Valued
data.description:
The stock of BKV Corporation (NYSE:BKV) has surged 19% in the last 12 months thanks to its strong business performance and a 23% rally of the price of natural gas. As this is a small-cap stock, it passes under the radar of most investors. However, it has promising growth prospects and a cheap valuation. As a result, the stock is attractive, despite its steep rally in the last 12 months.

Business Overview

BKV Corporation produces and sells natural gas in the Barnett Shale in the Fort Worth Basin of Texas and in the Marcellus Shale in the Appalachian Basin of Northeast Pennsylvania. It is also engaged in the activities of gathering, processing, and transporting natural gas.

BKV is the largest producer of natural gas in the Barnett Shale.

It recently acquired Bedrock for $370 million in cash, and thus it grew its production from 698 to 821 million cf/day. To provide a perspective, all the other gas producers in the area have a total output of approximately 1.2 billion cf/day. The assets of Bedrock include about 1 trillion cubic feet of reserves, and thus they are likely to prove a significant growth driver for BKV.

It is also important to note that there are many other acquisition opportunities in the area, in which BKV has a great record of acquiring and integrating assets into its system. As shown in the above slide, BKV has also acquired assets from Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Devon Energy (DVN) in the past, with management expecting to identify additional growth opportunities in this area in the future.

A key feature of BKV is its strong balance sheet, which enables the company to perform accretive acquisitions. The company has a low net leverage ratio of 0.63x and net debt (as per Buffett's formula: net debt = total liabilities - cash - receivables) of $635 million. This amount is only 35% of the market capitalization of the stock, and hence it is a testament to the strong financial position of the company. The healthy balance sheet of BKV is also a confirmation of the strong business model of the company, which does not require the issuance of a high debt load to fund the growth projects.

In the most recent quarter, BKV grew its production 2% over the prior year's quarter thanks to the development of new assets, which more than offset the natural decline of the fields of the company.

Notably, the actual output of 811 million cf/day exceeded the high end of the initial guidance of 775-805 million cf per day of management. As a result, management raised its guidance for the annual production rate to 790-810 million cf per day, signaling 4% growth at the mid-point.

Thanks to its strong business performance and favorable gas prices, BKV switched from a loss per share of -$0.34 to a profit per share of $0.39, thus exceeding the analysts' estimates by $0.24. Given the positive business momentum of BKV, analysts expect the company to post earnings per share of $1.27 this year and grow them by 43% in 2026 and 16% in 2027. If BKV meets the analysts' estimates, it will achieve earnings per share of $1.81 next year and $2.10 in 2027.

It is also remarkable that the outlook of the U.S. natural gas market is positive. According to the latest report of the Energy Information Administration [EIA], the total natural gas production is expected to remain essentially flat in 2026, while the total consumption is likely to somewhat increase thanks to higher LNG exports. As a result, EIA expects the average price of natural gas to surge 47% next year, from $3.40 in 2025 to $3.90. Of course, the forecast is surrounded by some inevitable uncertainty, as weather conditions significantly affect the consumption of natural gas. Nevertheless, the base scenario, which assumes normal weather conditions, is highly positive for BKV. Overall, thanks to sustained growth of production and a favorable outlook for gas prices, BKV is expected by analysts to grow its earnings per share at a fast pace in the upcoming years.

Valuation

BKV is currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 16.7x, which is much higher than the median earnings multiple of 12.2x of the energy sector. However, as the company seems on track to grow its bottom line significantly in the upcoming years, it is important to realize that the stock is trading at only 11.8 times its expected earnings in 2026 and 10.2 times its expected earnings in 2027. Therefore, the stock appears cheaply valued relative to its growth prospects, particularly given its strong balance sheet.

On the one hand, the cheap valuation can be attributed to the small market capitalization of $1.8 billion of the stock, which may be indicative of somewhat higher risk. On the other hand, BKV has a positive record of growing via acquisitions in its focus area and seems to have ample room for future growth. Therefore, the stock is likely to highly reward the investors who have a long-term perspective.

Risk

While the promising growth prospects, the cheap valuation, and the solid financial position of BKV bode well for the future returns of the stock, investors should be aware of the high sensitivity of the natural gas industry to weather conditions. An abnormally mild winter can cause gas inventories to surge and remain elevated for a long period. Consequently, abnormally mild weather can exert pressure on the price of natural gas for a considerable period. Therefore, the stock is suitable only for the investors who can endure periods of mild weather and stock price pressure and remain focused on the long-term prospects of the stock.

Final Thoughts

BKV passes under the radar of most investors due to its small market capitalization and its mundane business model. However, the stock appears attractive for the aforementioned reasons. It is also worth noting that natural gas is considered much cleaner than oil products, and hence it is essentially immune to the ongoing shift of many countries from fossil fuels to clean energy sources. While the price of natural gas is always sensitive to weather conditions, BKV is likely to highly reward the investors with a long-term horizon.

data.title: Parnassus Value Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

After a strong second quarter, when markets recovered from the sell-off related to tariff announcements in April, the Russell 1000 Value Index markets continued to power higher, posting strong returns for the quarter. Investor sentiment was bolstered by the ongoing megatrend in artificial intelligence (AI), relatively stable economic data and solid corporate earnings. The Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates for the first time in 2025, which offered another tailwind for the markets. Against this backdrop, the best- performing sectors in the Russell 1000 Value Index were Communication Services, Utilities and Consumer Discretionary, while the Consumer Staples sector ended the quarter with a slight loss.

Performance Review

Information Technology holdings boosted relative performance

The Fund returned 7.46% (net of fees) for the quarter, outperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index’s 5.33%. The outperformance was notably driven by our IT holdings, many of which were long-term holdings in the portfolio, buoyed by positive sentiment toward the latest AI developments. The relative gains were offset partially by holdings in the Materials and Health Care sectors.

For the year-to-date period, the Fund returned 12.95% (net of fees), outperforming the Russell 1000 Value’s 11.65%. The portfolio’s holdings in IT boosted its relative performance, while holdings in Health Care and Industrials detracted.

Top Contributors

Western Digital (WDC), a leading data storage products maker, advanced after the company's strong quarterly results. The AI megatrend is fueling demand for high-capacity, cost- effective storage.

Cummins (CMI) rallied after the engines and industrial equipment maker reported better-than-expected second-quarter results due to strong profitability in the distribution and power systems segment.

Oracle (ORCL) stock benefited from the firm's bullish outlook for its cloud services segment. The software and cloud infrastructure provider continues to benefit from the AI megatrend, signaling that cloud growth will accelerate.

Bank of New York Mellon (BK), one of the largest custodians in the world, advanced amid strong earnings results. Revenue growth continues to outpace expense growth, driving solid operating leverage across the company.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) benefited from growing demand for AI chips and its dominant position as the leading-edge contract foundry.

Bottom Contributors

Deere (DE) stock underperformed due to the company's reduced full-year guidance, citing factors like lower grain prices impacting farmer spending, tariff uncertainty and cautious customer spending.

S&P Global (SPGI) stock fell as a weak earnings report from a competitor raised fears about a potential slowdown in client spending within the financial information and analytics industry.

Novo Nordisk (NVO) shares declined after the company issued a profit warning amid rising competition in the obesity drug market. We maintain our conviction due to the company’s long-term pipeline, with potential catalysts in its oral GLP-1 launch.

Ball (BALL), a supplier of aluminum packaging, fell as investors worried over narrowing profit margins due to the shift in consumer tastes away from alcoholic beverages.

NICE (NICE), a software company providing contact center solutions, lost investor confidence due to concerns over AI disruption. However, we believe that the company should see AI transform their business model and drive long-term growth.

Portfolio Positioning

Rebalancing portfolio to take advantage of market opportunities

The portfolio continues to maintain its balanced, quality-anchored positioning between economically sensitive and defensive sectors. This results from our disciplined investment process, which pays equal attention to both risks and opportunities with the goal of delivering attractive risk-adjusted returns for long-term investors

Relative to the benchmark, our largest sector overweights are in the Financials, IT and Health Care sectors. The Fund's three largest sector underweights are Energy (no exposure), Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples.

In Financials, we maintain well-diversified exposure to both economically sensitive industries like banks and brokerages, as well as defensive ones like exchanges and insurance. The majority of our Financials exposure, however, is tied to companies that do not take significant lending risk and should fare relatively well if we see an economic downturn.

Within IT, we reduced our exposure modestly as many of our long-term holdings in the sector experienced solid price appreciation driven by growing investor optimism toward latest AI developments.

Within Health Care, we increased our overweight with the purchase of Abbott (ABT) and Stryker (SYK) to reinitiate exposure to the medical devices space. We believe both Abbott and Stryker have durable top- and bottom-line drivers underpinned by new product launches that’ll enable market share gains in several attractive end markets.

We increased our underweight in the Consumer Discretionary sector with the exit of Airbnb (ABNB). We now have lower conviction in Airbnb’s long-term thesis as noncore initiatives could create a structural drag on margins with the company’s expansion outside its core vacation rentals market. We also swapped our housing subsector exposure with the sale of homebuilder D.R. Horton (DHI) to purchase shares in Home Depot. We believe a combination of home equity wealth accumulation, mortgage lock-in effects and aging housing stock create multiyear tailwinds for home improvement companies.

Outlook

Elevated market valuations favor outperformance for value stocks

As we enter the fourth quarter, equity markets appear on track to deliver three consecutive years of double-digit returns. Resilient consumer spending, lower-than-expected impact from tariffs and robust investment in AI infrastructure continue to drive earnings growth for companies. Moreover, interest rate cuts and tax benefits from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act could further lower the likelihood of an imminent economic slowdown. While these tailwinds are real, equity valuations, by most measures, are also at all-time highs, leaving the current market rally susceptible to unexpected shocks.

We believe the current market environment favors value stocks to outperform. Stable economic growth, declining interest rates and high starting valuations for broad market indices create conditions for reasonably valued stocks to outperform those priced for higher earnings growth. While we cannot foretell the precise trajectory of economic growth, we remain steadfast in maintaining a margin of safety through our valuation discipline and investing in quality businesses that can thrive across a multitude of macroeconomic conditions. Integral to assessing both the valuation and quality of our investments is our bottom-up assessment of a company’s competitive moat, relevancy or growth prospects, management competence and sustainability characteristics. Additionally, our long-term orientation allows the full benefits of the strategic choices that our portfolio companies are making to play out in business fundamentals and eventually be reflected in share prices.

This consistent approach anchored in quality, valuation and a long-term investment horizon has helped us deliver attractive returns for 20 years since the Fund’s inception in 2005. We are confident that the collection of businesses we own in the portfolio today embodies this time-tested approach and positions us to continue delivering attractive returns in the years ahead.

data.title: BAGY: Monthly Income From Bitcoin Volatility At Double-Digit Yield
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Turning non-income-producing assets into cash flow generators is possible by using derivatives. In particular, selling options is a popular way to do this, and many funds have become available these days to do just that. A popular asset these days is Bitcoin (BTC-USD), and it was only a matter of time before call writing funds focused on Bitcoin came to the market.

Today, we'll be taking a look at the Amplify Bitcoin Max Income Covered Call ETF (BATS:BAGY). The fund is quite fresh, with an inception of April 29, 2025, but this isn't the first of its kind in this space. We'll be comparing this fund to several of these that we looked at previously.

BAGY Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: 3.33% SEC yield, 33.84% Distribution Yield
  • Expense Ratio: 0.65%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $16.433 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

BAGY "seeks to maximize current income through a covered call strategy tied to the investment exposure to the price return of Bitcoin." To achieve this, they use a relatively short option writing strategy: "BAGY seeks 30-60% annualized option premium, while also offering upside potential, by leveraging weekly options-writing techniques to harness volatility associated with the price of Bitcoin, transforming it into valuable income opportunities."

Peer Comparison

We've covered a couple of these call-writing focused Bitcoin funds previously, which include NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI), YieldMax Bitcoin Option Income Strategy ETF (YBIT) and Roundhill Bitcoin Covered Call Strategy ETF (YBTC). These seem to be some of the more popular fund sponsors of this new crop of derivative ETFs. BTCI has accumulated AUM of ~$844 million with 6.02k followers on Seeking Alpha, YBIT is at almost $131 million with 2.28k followers, and YBTC is at around $329 million and 3.43k followers.

BAGY is at almost $16.5 million and has only 568 followers that are noted on the SA ticker page. Which makes it much smaller and less followed, at least for now—and to be fair, the fund came to market only recently. The others launched in 2024, with YBIT and YBTC launching in early 2024 and BTCI launching in fall 2024. Still, it is quite clear that BTCI has taken the lead in terms of AUM, and YBIT has actually stalled, it seems, in garnering further AUM.

That is even despite Bitcoin rocketing higher this year, which would help to offset some of those declines through higher underlying asset value, increasing managed assets. That plays out in the performance of the underlying funds here, which have all seen strong gains, but some have seen better gains than others.

In this case, it has been YBIT that has seen the weakest results. We are restricted to the max total return charts going back to BAGY's inception, and being so new, it is only around 6 months' worth of data. At the same time, we can still see that all these other funds have performed incredibly well. Even enough to keep up with the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) performance in this period.

Of course, this is due to the different strategies that these funds take, as they don't all operate the same, and that's why we'll see some slightly different performance. In particular, the amount a fund is overweighted on their notional value of their portfolio and how far out-of-the-money they are writing the call options plays a critical role here.

Removing BAGY, we get more data to play with, and we start to see some clear divergences. YBIT remains the weakest performer by far, but we see all start to now lag IBIT—showing us that upside cap that can happen for call writing funds when an underlying asset is rallying so strongly.

Going back to the fund we are specifically discussing today, BAGY goes the synthetic long route on Bitcoin. That means it goes long calls and sells puts on longer-dated options. That creates a synthetic long exposure to the movement of Bitcoin. At the same time, the prospectus leaves the door open to investing in Bitcoin ETPs—they just don't appear to have any listed in their holdings at the time of writing.

In addition to its investments in Bitcoin ETPs, the Fund expects to also obtain exposure to the Bitcoin Price by buying and selling a combination of Bitcoin ETP Options that reference a Bitcoin ETP or the Bitcoin ETP Index. In general, put options give the holder (i.e., the buyer) the right to sell an asset (or deliver the cash value of the asset, in case of certain put options) and the seller (i.e., the writer) of the put has the obligation to buy the asset (or receive cash value of the asset, in case of certain put options) at a certain defined price (the “strike price”). Call options give the holder (i.e., the buyer) the right to buy an asset (or receive cash value of the asset, in case of certain call options) and the seller (i.e., the writer) the obligation to sell the asset (or deliver cash value of the asset, in case of certain call options) at a certain defined strike price. The Fund’s options exposure to the Bitcoin ETPs is considered to be “synthetic.” The synthetic exposure may be created through the combination of purchasing call options and selling put options generally in the same amount, at the same strike price with the same expiration. This combination synthetically creates the upside and downside participation in the Bitcoin Price, as represented by the Bitcoin ETP or Bitcoin ETP Index.

They then sit on larger cash balances that are in cash or cash equivalents. This generates some interest, but the primary 'income' is derived from selling the calls in the fund.

BAGY uses a similar approach to YBTC and YBIT with this strategy. BTCI also does something similar with creating synthetic long positions, but they also take around a ~25% stake in Bitcoin ETFs as well. One of those includes IBIT, currently at 1.14%, but also VanEck Bitcoin ETF (HODL) to a larger degree at 23.54%.

So where the big difference we are going to see play out is in how overwritten and aggressive they are in terms of writing OTM, or at-the-money options. YieldMax will be the most aggressive here, in an effort to generate the max 'yield'—which is kind of what the fund sponsor is known for and the name would imply.

At the same time, BAGY's distribution rate comes in at 33.84%, BTCI is at 28.07%, YBIT is at 35.54%, and YBTC comes in at 47.02%. So we aren't necessarily seeing YBIT even delivering the highest distribution. Of course, the caveat is that these distribution rates will change frequently as each monthly or weekly payout is made—YBTC went with a weekly distribution schedule, which can be more appealing for some investors.

Each fund will be positioned a bit differently at any given time, but in general, the other fund sponsors allow more flexibility in how overwritten the portfolio is. BAGY notes that they will write calls "that are approximately 5% out of the money" with targeted maturities of one week or less." That one week or less can be important because the fund intends to overwrite 100% of its portfolio. That means that, assuming the strategy is employed exactly as written, anything over a 5% rise in Bitcoin in roughly a week would be forfeited, as it would be capped.

In targeting strike prices that are 5% “out-of-the-money” on a weekly basis the Fund should participate in the upside on the first 5% rise in share price of the Bitcoin ETPs. Beyond the first 5% increase the Fund would not participate in the upside because 100% of the Bitcoin ETP shares are expected to be covered.

YBTC and YBIT are nearly 100% overwritten as well, and that is where BTCI is a bit different, as their overwritten allocation fluctuates. At the time of writing my YBTC piece, they were at roughly 55%—with today being quite similar at around 53.9%. Additionally, BTCI uses a laddered approach to writing calls, meaning that the fund isn't capped if the first strike price is breached on the lower strike leg. That all helps to limit upside cap, but the fund does write longer-dated options with the November 21 expiration.

YBTC also writes options that are out a bit further, but only going out to October 17, or what is roughly two weeks at this point. That's where BAGY is a bit different in writing options, with expirations only a week or less.

Conclusion

BAGY is a newer offering in the Bitcoin call writing space, and it looks to have performed similarly to peers in its short life thus far. However, it is quite similar to YBTC in the way it employs its options writing strategy. If that is any guide, then BTCI could continue to be the king of the Bitcoin call writing space. However, this comes with a caveat that it will remain the better performer as long as Bitcoin's price moves higher at a brisk pace. In a sideways or down Bitcoin market, it should, given the design of the fund's strategy, underperform.

BAGY, with its short-dated calls, is in a position to capture quite a bit of upside as well since it can tweak the strike price on a more regular basis. That's one of the appeals that I've noted in some 0DTE option funds that have made their way to the market as well—though it isn't without its own flaws. I'll be interested to watch how BAGY starts to perform over time relative to their peers.
